result:
The first thing that comes is the sound of rain on a plastic roof, the way it makes everything feel temporary, like we’re living inside a tent someone forgot to pack away. I’m not sure why that sound matters more than, say, the hum of the refrigerator or the neighbor’s dog that barks at ghosts every 3:12 a.m., but it does. It’s the soundtrack to the moment I realized memory isn’t a library but a bucket with a hole in it; the level keeps dropping and we keep calling what’s left “the truth.”

I used to think 1000 words was a fence. Now it feels like a passport: enough pages to go somewhere, not enough to stay. So let’s cross while the guards are sleepy. Let’s start with the smell of cut grass in May, the way it floods the nose like green nostalgia, even if you grew up in a city where lawns were myths told by suburban cousins. That smell is a time machine built of chlorophyll and sunlight, and it drops me behind a house I haven’t seen since I was nine, where my grandmother kept three rusted nails in a jam jar because “you never know.” She spoke in puzzles; I’ve spent decades assembling the pieces and still can’t tell whether she was预言ing or just conserving syllables.

Traveling farther, I land on the first lie I ever told: “I didn’t break it.” The object was a porcelain swan, neck elegant as a question mark, wings spread mid-flight even while glued to the mantel. I swatted it with a foam lightsaber, a neon clash of mythologies. When the neck snapped, the silence felt heavier than any scolding. I learned that guilt has a frequency, like dog whistles, and adults pretend not to hear it but they do; they just answer with new chores. I buried the shards in the backyard underneath the lemon tree that never produced fruit. That’s where I also buried the idea that truth protects you. Sometimes soil is just another drawer we don’t open anymore.

The lemon tree didn’t fruit, but the neighbors’ peaches dangled like sunset lanterns. One July I stole one—an act of war in the suburbs. Bit into it behind the garage and tasted stolen sunshine, woolly and warm. Juice ran down my arm like liquid evidence. I waited for the sky to fall; instead, the neighbor simply asked if I’d seen any squirrels “behaving suspiciously.” I nodded, gave the squirrels an alibi, and walked away believing in minor mercies and the collaborative fiction we call community.

Fast-forward through scraped knees, mixtapes, and the first time my heart shattered so loudly I thought the fire alarm would go off. It didn’t. The world is under no obligation to echo your interiors. That’s why we write, paint, dance, text exes at 2:07 a.m.—trying to get the universe to flinch first. I stitched the shards into a mosaic that looked like ambition, applied to colleges I couldn’t afford, and learned that debt is just anticipation with interest.

College smelled like microwave popcorn and ambition. I majored in pretending to read Russian novels, minored in loving people who used me as a comma in the long sentence of their life—necessary, but never the point. One professor told me the secret of literature is that every book is really about time. Even the porno-script of capitalism squatting on bestseller lists: time equals money. Even the cookbooks: time equals dinner. Even the blank journal you gave your ex: time equals absence. I wrote that on the back of a napkin and lost it in a laundromat. Someone somewhere is folding underwear with my half-eaten wisdom.

Afterward, I lived in seven cities in eight years, collecting street names like stamps. Every move felt like rehearsing for a play that never opened. I learned to say “goodbye” in ten languages; each tongue left a different scar. The trick is to keep the suitcases small and the memories smaller. Yet the mind is rebellious: it expands what you try to compress. I still taste the kimchi in Seoul at 4 a.m., still hear the accordion in Montmartre played by a man with only three fingers, still feel the sting of salt in Caye Caulker when a stranger told me “everyone floats here, even the turtles know when to rest.” I carried those moments like contraband across borders, afraid customs would confiscate the only wealth I had.

Once, on a delayed train outside Zagreb, a woman lent me her phone charger. We spoke no common language except percentages climbing back into the green. When my screen lit, she grinned like we’d co-authored resurrection. Then the train moved, she got off, never exchanged names. I think about her whenever someone says technology isolates us. I think about the way she cradled that cable like a stethoscope, listening the heart of a stranger back to life.

I’ve lost jobs the way others lose umbrellas: suddenly, mysteriously, often during sunshine. Each dismissal felt like eviction from a future I’d pre-furnished. Yet every time I stumbled into the next thing—selling books, writing ads, teaching poems to teenagers who preferred memes—I found a new room in the house of myself. The secret is to keep walking, even when the hallway shrinks. The corridor only looks narrow because you’re carrying yesterday’s measurements.

Once, during a hurricane, I watched a man jog down the boardwalk in a business suit, briefcase in hand, rain sideways, wind howling like unpaid debts. He looked like he was commuting to Armageddon. I still don’t know if that was denial or discipline, but I admired the suit. We are all running to meetings that may or may not exist, calendars filled with imaginary ink. The difference is whether we notice the water rising.

At some point I decided love was a kind of translation: never exact, always lossy, but sometimes the only raft. I married a woman who laughs at my contradictions, who proofreads my silences. We bought a house with a plastic roof. When it rains, we brew tea and sit under the percussion, pretending we’re homeless in a good way, unburdened by permanence. She still asks why I keep a jam jar with three rusted nails. I tell her you never know. We smile at the circularity of inheritance.

We planted a lemon tree. It actually fruits, small planets of sour. I cut one open and buried the seeds in the backyard, just in case. Maybe someone will need a future that smells like May.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
